infobox.key: • Exploration of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") begins with [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier").

• [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") integrates New France into the royal domain, endows it with a [new administration](/wiki/Sovereign_Council_of_New_France "Sovereign Council of New France") and founds the [French West India Company](/wiki/French_West_India_Company "French West India Company").

• By the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht"), [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") cedes most of [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") to the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") as well as its claims on [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)") and [Hudson's Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay").

• By the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") cedes New France to [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").

**New France**[[d]](#cite_note-10) was the [territory](/wiki/Territory "Territory") colonized by [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), beginning with the exploration of the [Gulf of Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_Saint_Lawrence "Gulf of Saint Lawrence") by [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") in 1534 and ending with the cession of New France to [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700%E2%80%931808) "History of Spain (1700–1808)") in 1763 under the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)").

A vast [viceroyalty](/wiki/Viceroyalty "Viceroyalty"), New France consisted of five colonies at its peak in 1712, each with its own administration: [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)"), the most developed colony, which was divided into the districts of Quebec (around what is now called [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City")), [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières"), and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"); [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"); [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") in the northeast; [Terre-Neuve](/wiki/Terre-Neuve_(New_France) "Terre-Neuve (New France)") on the island of [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"); and [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)").[[7]](#cite_note-Francis2009-11)[[8]](#cite_note-Territoire-12) It extended from Newfoundland to the [Canadian Prairies](/wiki/Canadian_Prairies "Canadian Prairies") and from Hudson Bay to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), including all the [Great Lakes of North America](/wiki/Great_Lakes_of_North_America "Great Lakes of North America"). The continent-traversing [Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") and [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") rivers were means of carrying French influence through much of North America.

In the 16th century, the lands were used primarily to extract natural resources, such as furs, through trade with the various indigenous peoples. In the seventeenth century, successful settlements began in Acadia and in Quebec. In the [1713 Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Peace_of_Utrecht "Peace of Utrecht"), France ceded to Great Britain its claims over mainland Acadia, Hudson Bay, and Newfoundland. France established the colony of [Île Royale](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-Royale_(New_France) "Île-Royale (New France)") on [Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island"), where they built the [Fortress of Louisbourg](/wiki/Fortress_of_Louisbourg "Fortress of Louisbourg").[[9]](#cite_note-Johnston2001-13)[[10]](#cite_note-Louisbourg-14)

The population rose slowly but steadily. In 1754, New France's population consisted of 10,000 [Acadians](/wiki/Acadian "Acadian"), 55,000 *[Canadiens](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians")*, and about 4,000 settlers in [upper and lower Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"); 69,000 in total.[[11]](#cite_note-jacques-15) The British expelled the Acadians in the [Great Upheaval](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians") from 1755 to 1764, and their descendants are dispersed in the [Maritime provinces](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes") of Canada and in [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") and [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), with small populations in [Chéticamp, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Ch%C3%A9ticamp,_Nova_Scotia "Chéticamp, Nova Scotia"), and the [Magdalen Islands](/wiki/Magdalen_Islands#History "Magdalen Islands"). Some also went to France.

After the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") (which included the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") in America), France ceded the rest of New France to Great Britain and Spain in the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") of 1763, although fishing rights around Newfoundland were retained. To assist with those fishing rights, Great Britain returned the islands of [Saint Pierre and Miquelon](/wiki/Saint_Pierre_and_Miquelon "Saint Pierre and Miquelon"), which had been lost in 1713. Britain acquired Canada, Acadia, and French Louisiana east of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), except for the [Île d'Orléans](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans,_Louisiana "Île d'Orléans, Louisiana"), which was granted to Spain with the territory to the west. In 1800, Spain returned [its portion of Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)") to France under the secret [Treaty of San Ildefonso](/wiki/Third_Treaty_of_San_Ildefonso "Third Treaty of San Ildefonso"), and [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") sold it to the United States in the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") of 1803, permanently ending French colonial efforts on the North American mainland.

New France eventually became absorbed within the United States and Canada, with the only vestige of French rule being the tiny islands of [Saint Pierre and Miquelon](/wiki/Saint_Pierre_and_Miquelon "Saint Pierre and Miquelon"), an [overseas collectivity](/wiki/Overseas_collectivity "Overseas collectivity") of [France](/wiki/France "France"), although Quebec remains predominantly French-speaking. In the United States, the legacy of New France includes [numerous place names](/wiki/List_of_place_names_of_French_origin_in_the_United_States "List of place names of French origin in the United States") as well as small pockets of [French-speaking](/wiki/French_language_in_the_United_States "French language in the United States") communities.

The 1715 [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Peace_of_Utrecht "Peace of Utrecht") resulted in the relinquishing of French claims to mainland Acadia, the Hudson Bay and Newfoundland, and the establishment of the colony of [Île Royale](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island"), now called Cape Breton Island, where the French built the [Fortress of Louisbourg](/wiki/Fortress_of_Louisbourg "Fortress of Louisbourg").[[9]](#cite_note-Johnston2001-13)[[13]](#cite_note-17)

### Growth of the settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Growth of the settlements")]

Further information: [Population of Canada § Former colonies and territories](/wiki/Population_of_Canada#Former_colonies_and_territories "Population of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Jean_Talon_illustration_from_The_Great_Intendant.jpg/250px-Jean_Talon_illustration_from_The_Great_Intendant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean_Talon_illustration_from_The_Great_Intendant.jpg)

[Jean Talon](/wiki/Jean_Talon "Jean Talon"), count of Orsainville, first intendant of New France

The European population grew slowly under French rule,[[14]](#cite_note-Preston2009b-18) thus remained relatively low as growth was largely achieved through natural births, rather than by immigration.[[15]](#cite_note-Powell2009t-19) Most of the French were farmers, and the rate of natural increase among the settlers themselves was very high.[[16]](#cite_note-McIlwraithMuller2001-20) The women had about 30 per cent more children than their counterparts who remained in France.[[17]](#cite_note-Landry-21) Yves Landry says, "Canadians had an exceptional diet for their time."[[18]](#cite_note-22) The [1666 census of New France](/wiki/1666_census_of_New_France "1666 census of New France") was the first census conducted in North America.[[19]](#cite_note-new-23) It was organized by [Jean Talon](/wiki/Jean_Talon "Jean Talon"), the first [Intendant of New France](/wiki/Intendant_of_New_France "Intendant of New France"), between 1665 and 1666.[[19]](#cite_note-new-23) According to Talon's census there were 3,215 people in New France, comprising 538 separate families.[[20]](#cite_note-Talon-24) The census showed a great difference in the number of men at 2,034 versus 1,181 women.[[20]](#cite_note-Talon-24) The French government sought to rectify this over the next few years by sending approximately 800 unmarried women, known as the *[Filles du Roi](/wiki/Filles_du_Roi "Filles du Roi")* ("King's Daughters"), to the colony.

By the early 1700s, the New France settlers were well established along the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") and [Acadian Peninsula](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_peninsula#Historic_name "Nova Scotia peninsula") with a population around 15,000-16,000.[[21]](#cite_note-25) The first population figures for Acadia are from 1671, which enumerated only 450 people.[[22]](#cite_note-auto-26)

After the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713, New France began to prosper. Industries such as fishing and farming, which had failed under Talon, began to flourish. A "King's Highway" (*[Chemin du Roy](/wiki/Chemin_du_Roy "Chemin du Roy")*) was built between Montreal and Quebec to encourage faster trade. The shipping industry also flourished as new ports were built and old ones were upgraded. The number of colonists greatly increased. By 1720, Canada had become a self-sufficient colony with a population of 24,594.[[23]](#cite_note-Belanger-27)
Mainly due to natural increase and modest immigration from [Northwest France](/wiki/Regions_of_France "Regions of France") ([Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"), [Île-de-France](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-de-France "Île-de-France"), [Poitou-Charentes](/wiki/Poitou-Charentes "Poitou-Charentes") and [Pays de la Loire](/wiki/Pays_de_la_Loire "Pays de la Loire")) the population of Canada increased to 55,000 according to the last French census of 1754.[[24]](#cite_note-Leslie1988a-28) This was an increase from 42,701 in 1730.[[25]](#cite_note-Hartz1969-29) By 1765, the population approached 70,000.[[23]](#cite_note-Belanger-27)

By 1714, the Acadian population had expanded to over 2,500, and by the end of the 1750s it had reached about 13,000.[[22]](#cite_note-auto-26) This was mostly from natural increase rather than immigration that affected other French settlements.[[22]](#cite_note-auto-26)

The European population of Louisiana is estimated at 5,000 by the 1720s.[[26]](#cite_note-Works1891-30) However, by the mid-1730s the colony had suffered the loss of 2,000 French settlers, though it added African slaves.[[27]](#cite_note-LescarbotBiggar1907-31) Enslaved men, women and children represented approximately 65 percent of the 6,000 non-indigenous population of Louisiana by the end of French rule.[[27]](#cite_note-LescarbotBiggar1907-31)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Early exploration (1523–1650s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early exploration (1523–1650s)")]

Around 1523, the [Florentine](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence") navigator [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano") convinced King [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") to commission an expedition to find a western route to [Cathay](/wiki/Cathay "Cathay") (China).[[28]](#cite_note-32) Late that year, Verrazzano set sail in [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe,_Seine-Maritime "Dieppe, Seine-Maritime"), crossing the Atlantic on a small [caravel](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel") with 50 men.[[29]](#cite_note-CoxAlbala2009-33) After exploring the coast of the present-day [Carolinas](/wiki/The_Carolinas "The Carolinas") early the following year, he headed north along the coast, eventually anchoring in the [Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows") of [New York Bay](/wiki/New_York_Bay "New York Bay").[[29]](#cite_note-CoxAlbala2009-33)

The first European to visit the site of present-day New York, Verrazzano named it [Nouvelle-Angoulême](/wiki/New_Angoul%C3%AAme "New Angoulême") in honour of the [king](/wiki/King "King"), the former count of [Angoulême](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme "Angoulême").[[30]](#cite_note-34) Verrazzano's voyage convinced the king to seek to establish a colony in the newly discovered land. Verrazzano gave the names *Francesca* and *Nova Gallia* to that land between [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") (Mexico) and English Newfoundland.[[31]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Samuel_de_Champlain_Carte_geographique_de_la_Nouvelle_France.jpg/500px-Samuel_de_Champlain_Carte_geographique_de_la_Nouvelle_France.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samuel_de_Champlain_Carte_geographique_de_la_Nouvelle_France.jpg)

A map of New France made by [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") in 1612

In 1534, [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") planted a cross in the [Gaspé Peninsula](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9_Peninsula "Gaspé Peninsula") and claimed the land in the name of [King Francis I](/wiki/King_Francis_I "King Francis I").[[32]](#cite_note-Riendeau-36)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] It was the first province of New France. The first settlement of 400 people, Fort [Charlesbourg-Royal](/wiki/Charlesbourg-Royal "Charlesbourg-Royal") (present-day [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City")), was attempted in 1541 but lasted only two years.[[33]](#cite_note-37)

French fishing fleets continued to sail to the Atlantic coast and into the St. Lawrence River, making alliances with Canadian [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") that became important once France began to occupy the land. French merchants soon realized the St. Lawrence region was full of valuable [fur](/wiki/Fur "Fur")-bearing animals, especially the [beaver](/wiki/American_Beaver "American Beaver"), which were becoming rare in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). Eventually, the French crown decided to colonize the territory to secure and expand its influence in America.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") and [Canada (New France)](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") were inhabited by [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_North_America "Indigenous peoples of North America") nomadic [Algonquian peoples](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") and sedentary [Iroquoian](/wiki/Iroquoian "Iroquoian") peoples. These lands were full of unexploited and valuable natural resources, which attracted all of Europe. By the 1580s, French trading companies had been set up, and ships were contracted to bring back furs. Much of what transpired between the indigenous population and their European visitors around that time is not known, for lack of historical records.[[32]](#cite_note-Riendeau-36)

Other attempts at establishing permanent settlements were also failures. In 1598, a French trading post was established on [Sable Island](/wiki/Sable_Island "Sable Island"), off the coast of Acadia, but was unsuccessful. In 1600, a trading post was established at [Tadoussac](/wiki/Tadoussac,_Quebec "Tadoussac, Quebec"), but only five settlers survived the winter.[[32]](#cite_note-Riendeau-36)

#### Foundation and development of Port Royal (1605)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foundation and development of Port Royal (1605)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Habitation_Port-Royal_-_Champlain.jpg/250px-Habitation_Port-Royal_-_Champlain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Habitation_Port-Royal_-_Champlain.jpg)

Habitation at Port-Royal c. 1605

In 1604, [King Henri IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") sponsored [Pierre Dugua, Sieur de Mons](/wiki/Pierre_Dugua,_Sieur_de_Mons "Pierre Dugua, Sieur de Mons") to found a settlement in New France. It was initially located at [Île-Saint-Croix](/wiki/Saint_Croix_Island,_Maine "Saint Croix Island, Maine") in modern-day [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), which was moved to [Port-Royal](/wiki/Habitation_at_Port-Royal "Habitation at Port-Royal"), Acadia, in modern day [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), in 1605.[[32]](#cite_note-Riendeau-36) Early participants in Port Royal included [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") and [Jean de Biencourt de Poutrincourt et de Saint-Just.](/wiki/Jean_de_Biencourt_de_Poutrincourt_et_de_Saint-Just "Jean de Biencourt de Poutrincourt et de Saint-Just")

The [first seigneury in New France](/wiki/Lordship_of_Port-Royal "Lordship of Port-Royal"), was granted to Poutrincourt in 1604, who actively promoted its development. By 1607, New France's first grist mill was established on the seigneury.[[34]](#cite_note-38) Poutrincourt invited [Louis Hébert](/wiki/Louis_H%C3%A9bert "Louis Hébert") to Port Royal where he pioneered many European agricultural practices in the New World, including [viticulture](/wiki/Viticulture "Viticulture"), [arboriculture](/wiki/Arboriculture "Arboriculture") and [cereal farming](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal"), before relocating to Quebec in 1617.

The principal structure of early Port Royal, the [habitation](/wiki/Port-Royal_National_Historic_Site "Port-Royal National Historic Site"), was destroyed in 1613, after which settlers moved to other nearby locations.[[32]](#cite_note-Riendeau-36) Port Royal was the first successful attempt by Europeans to establish a permanent settlement in New France.[[35]](#cite_note-39) Although the French, notably [Charles de Saint-Étienne de la Tour](/wiki/Charles_de_Saint-%C3%89tienne_de_la_Tour "Charles de Saint-Étienne de la Tour"), remained active after 1613, official support for Port Royal resumed only in 1632. Control of Port Royal and Acadia shifted between Britain and France until Britain permanently took it in 1712. The Acadians remained dominant until their [deportation began in 1755](/wiki/Bay_of_Fundy_campaign "Bay of Fundy campaign"). Allowed to return in 1764 if they swore allegiance,[[36]](#cite_note-40) Acadians today form a significant French-speaking community in New Brunswick and Nova Scotia.

#### Foundation of Quebec City (1608)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Foundation of Quebec City (1608)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Champlain_Habitation_de_Quebec.jpg/250px-Champlain_Habitation_de_Quebec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Champlain_Habitation_de_Quebec.jpg)

Habitation at Quebec c. 1608

In 1608, King Henry IV sponsored Pierre Dugua, Sieur de Mons and Samuel de Champlain as founders of [the city of Quebec](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") with 28 men. This was the second permanent French settlement in the colony of [Canada](/wiki/Canada,_New_France "Canada, New France").[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43) Colonization was slow and difficult. Many settlers died early because of harsh weather and diseases. In 1630, there were only 103 colonists living in the settlement, but by 1640, the population had reached 355.[[40]](#cite_note-44)

Champlain allied himself with the [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") and [Montagnais](/wiki/Innu "Innu") peoples in the area, who were at war with the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), as soon as possible. In 1609, Champlain and two French companions accompanied his Algonquin, Montagnais, and [Huron](/wiki/Wendat_people "Wendat people") allies south from the St. Lawrence Valley to [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain"). He participated decisively in a battle against the Iroquois there, killing two Iroquois chiefs with the first shot of his [arquebus](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus"). This military engagement against the Iroquois solidified Champlain's status with New France's Huron and Algonquin allies, enabling him to maintain bonds essential to New France's interests in the fur trade.[[41]](#cite_note-Douglas_Hunter_2000,_pp._240+-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Western_New_France%2C_1688.jpg/250px-Western_New_France%2C_1688.jpg)](/wiki/File:Western_New_France,_1688.jpg)

A map of western New France, including the [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"), by [Vincenzo Coronelli](/wiki/Vincenzo_Coronelli "Vincenzo Coronelli"), 1688

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/1592_4_Nova_Doetecum_mr.jpg/250px-1592_4_Nova_Doetecum_mr.jpg)](/wiki/File:1592_4_Nova_Doetecum_mr.jpg)

1592 map of New France by [Petrus Plancius](/wiki/Petrus_Plancius "Petrus Plancius")

Champlain also arranged to have young French men live with local indigenous people, to learn their language and customs and help the French adapt to life in North America. These *[coureurs des bois](/wiki/Coureurs_des_bois "Coureurs des bois")* ("runners of the woods"), including [Étienne Brûlé](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Br%C3%BBl%C3%A9 "Étienne Brûlé"), extended French influence south and west to the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") and among the Huron tribes who lived there. Ultimately, for the better part of a century, the Iroquois and French clashed in a series of attacks and reprisals.[[41]](#cite_note-Douglas_Hunter_2000,_pp._240+-45)

During the first decades of the colony's existence, only a few hundred French people lived there, while the [English colonies](/wiki/English_colonial_empire "English colonial empire") to the south were much more populous and wealthy. [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"), adviser to [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII"), wished to make New France as significant as the English colonies. In 1627, Richelieu founded the [Company of One Hundred Associates](/wiki/Company_of_One_Hundred_Associates "Company of One Hundred Associates") to invest in New France, promising land parcels to hundreds of new settlers and to turn Canada into an important mercantile and farming colony.[[42]](#cite_note-Knecht_1991_165-46) He named Champlain as the [Governor of New France](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France") and forbade non-[Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") to live there. Consequently, any [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") emigrants to New France were forced to convert to Catholicism, prompting many of them to relocate to the English colonies instead.[[42]](#cite_note-Knecht_1991_165-46)

The Catholic Church, and missionaries such as the [Recollets](/wiki/Recollets "Recollets") and the [Jesuits](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus"), became firmly established in the territory. Richelieu also expanded the [seigneurial system](/wiki/Seigneurial_system_of_New_France "Seigneurial system of New France") from its beginnings at [Port-Royal](/wiki/Lordship_of_Port-Royal "Lordship of Port-Royal"). This was a semi-feudal system of farming based on [ribbon farms](/wiki/Ribbon_farm "Ribbon farm") that remained a characteristic feature of the St. Lawrence valley until the 19th century. While Richelieu's efforts did little to increase the French presence in New France, they did pave the way for the success of later efforts.[[42]](#cite_note-Knecht_1991_165-46)

Simultaneously, during the [Anglo-French War (1627–1629)](/wiki/Anglo-French_War_(1627%E2%80%931629) "Anglo-French War (1627–1629)") the English colonies to the south began raiding the St. Lawrence Valley, Champlain surrendered Quebec on 19 July 1629.[[43]](#cite_note-kirkbio-47) The British held New France with Kirke as governor until 1632,[[44]](#cite_note-Michael-48) when [Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1632)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1632) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1632)") was signed on March 29, 1632, which returned New France ([Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") and [Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island")) to French control.[[44]](#cite_note-Michael-48) Champlain returned to Canada that year and requested that Sieur de Laviolette found another trading post at [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res,_Quebec "Trois-Rivières, Quebec"), which Laviolette did in 1634. Champlain died in 1635.

On 23 September 1646, under the command of [Pierre LeGardeur](/wiki/Pierre_Legardeur_de_Repentigny_(admiral) "Pierre Legardeur de Repentigny (admiral)"), Le Cardinal arrived to Quebec with Jules (Gilles) [Trottier II](/wiki/Trottier#The_first_Trottiers_in_Québec "Trottier") and his family. Le Cardinal, commissioned by the [Communauté des Habitants](/wiki/Company_of_Habitants "Company of Habitants"), had arrived from [La Rochelle, France](/wiki/La_Rochelle "La Rochelle"). Communauté des Habitants at the time of Trottier traded fur primarily. On 4 July 1646, by Pierre Teuleron, sieur de Repentigny, granted Trottier land in La Rochelle to build and develop New France, under the authorization [Jacques Le Neuf de la Poterie](/wiki/Jacques_Leneuf_de_La_Poterie "Jacques Leneuf de La Poterie").

### Royal takeover and attempts to settle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Royal takeover and attempts to settle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Naval_Flag_of_the_Kingdom_of_France_%28Civil_Ensign%29.svg/250px-Naval_Flag_of_the_Kingdom_of_France_%28Civil_Ensign%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Naval_Flag_of_the_Kingdom_of_France_(Civil_Ensign).svg)

The Merchant Flag of France (1689 design), inspiration for the [flag of Quebec](/wiki/Flag_of_Quebec "Flag of Quebec")

In 1650, New France had seven hundred colonists and Montreal had only a few dozen settlers. Because the First Nations people did most of the work of beaver hunting, the company needed few French employees. The sparsely populated New France almost fell to hostile Iroquois forces completely as well. In 1660, settler [Adam Dollard des Ormeaux](/wiki/Adam_Dollard_des_Ormeaux "Adam Dollard des Ormeaux") led a Canadian and Huron [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia") against a much larger Iroquois force; none of the Canadians survived, although they did turn back the Iroquois invasion.

In 1627, Quebec had only eighty-five French colonists and was easily overwhelmed two years later when three English privateers plundered the settlement. In 1663, New France finally became more secure when [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") made it a royal province, taking control away from the [Company of One Hundred Associates](/wiki/Company_of_One_Hundred_Associates "Company of One Hundred Associates"). In the same year the [Société Notre-Dame de Montréal](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Notre-Dame_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Société Notre-Dame de Montréal") ceded its possessions to the [Seminaire de Saint-Sulpice](/wiki/Society_of_Saint-Sulpice "Society of Saint-Sulpice").[[45]](#cite_note-49)

The Crown paid for transatlantic passages and offered other incentives to those willing to move to New France as well, after which the population of New France grew to three thousand.[[46]](#cite_note-50)

In 1665, Louis XIV sent a French garrison, the [Carignan-Salières Regiment](/wiki/Carignan-Sali%C3%A8res_Regiment "Carignan-Salières Regiment"), to Quebec. The colonial government was reformed along the lines of the government of France, with the Governor General and [Intendant](/wiki/Intendant_of_New_France "Intendant of New France") subordinate to the French Minister of the Marine. In 1665, [Jean Talon](/wiki/Jean_Talon "Jean Talon") Minister of the Marine accepted an appointment from [Jean-Baptiste Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert") as the first Intendant of New France. These reforms limited the power of the [Bishop of Quebec](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Quebec "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Quebec"), who had held the greatest amount of power after the death of Champlain.

Talon tried reforming the seigneurial system by forcing the *seigneurs* to reside on their land and limiting the size of the *seigneuries,* intending to make more land available to new settlers. Talon's attempts failed since very few settlers arrived and the various industries he established failed to surpass the importance of the fur trade.

#### Settlers and their families

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Settlers and their families")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/The_Arrival_of_the_French_Girls_at_Quebec%2C_1667_-_C.W._Jefferys.jpg/250px-The_Arrival_of_the_French_Girls_at_Quebec%2C_1667_-_C.W._Jefferys.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Arrival_of_the_French_Girls_at_Quebec,_1667_-_C.W._Jefferys.jpg)

One group of [King's Daughters](/wiki/King%27s_Daughters "King's Daughters") arrives at Quebec, 1667

The first settler, brought to Quebec by Champlain, was the apothecary [Louis Hébert](/wiki/Louis_H%C3%A9bert "Louis Hébert") and his family of Paris. They expressly came to settle and remain in New France so as to make the settlement viable. Waves of recruits came in response to the requests for men with specific skills, *e*.*g*., farmers, architects, and blacksmiths. At the same time, the government encouraged intermarriages with the indigenous peoples and welcomed [indentured servants](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude"), or *engagés* sent to New France. As couples married, cash incentives to have large families were put in place and proved effective.

To further strengthen the nascent [France's colonial empire](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire"), [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") sponsored single women, virtuous, physically fit, and aged between 15 and 30 years, known as the [King's Daughters](/wiki/King%27s_Daughters "King's Daughters"), or, in French, *les filles du roi*, to move to New France. The King paid for their passage and granted goods or money as their dowries upon their marriage to single settlers. Approximately 800 women, primarily from the impoverished Parisian, Norman, and West-Central families, relocated during 1663–1673. By 1672, the population of New France had risen to 6,700 people, a marked increase from the population of 3,200 people in 1663.[[47]](#cite_note-peupl-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/1664AmeriqueNord.jpg/330px-1664AmeriqueNord.jpg)](/wiki/File:1664AmeriqueNord.jpg)

Political map of the northeastern part of North America in 1664

This rapid demographic growth was predicated both on the high demand for children and on the ready supply of natural resources to support them. According to Landry, "Canadians had an exceptional diet for their time. This was due to the natural abundance of meat, fish, and pure water; the good food conservation conditions during the winter; and an adequate wheat supply in most years."[[17]](#cite_note-Landry-21) Consequently, colonial women bore about 30% more children than comparable women in France.

Besides household duties, some women participated in the fur trade, the major source of money in New France. They worked at home alongside their husbands or fathers as merchants, clerks, and provisioners. Some were widows who took over their husbands' roles. Some even became independent and active entrepreneurs.[[48]](#cite_note-52)

#### Settlements in Louisiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Settlements in Louisiana")]

Main article: [Louisiana (New France)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/New_orleans_plan_1728.jpg/250px-New_orleans_plan_1728.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_orleans_plan_1728.jpg)

Map of New Orleans in 1728

The French extended their territorial claim to the south and to the west of the [American colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") late in the 17th century, naming it for King Louis XIV, as [La Louisiane](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"). In 1682, [René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle") explored the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") Valley and the [Mississippi River Valley](/wiki/Mississippi_embayment "Mississippi embayment"), and he claimed the entire territory for [France](/wiki/Early_modern_France "Early modern France") as far south as the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").[[49]](#cite_note-Magocsi1999w-53)

La Salle attempted to establish the first southern colony in the new territory in 1685, but inaccurate maps and navigational issues led him to instead establish his [Fort Saint Louis](/wiki/French_colonization_of_Texas "French colonization of Texas") in what is now [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"). The colony was devastated by disease, and the surviving settlers were killed in 1688, in an attack by the area's [indigenous population](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas").[[50]](#cite_note-54) Other parts of Louisiana were settled and developed with success, such as [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") and [southern Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"), leaving a strong [French influence](/wiki/History_of_Louisiana#French_exploration_and_colonization_(1682–1763) "History of Louisiana") in these areas long after the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase").

Many strategic [forts](/wiki/Fort "Fort") were built there, under the orders of Governor [Louis de Buade de Frontenac](/wiki/Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac "Louis de Buade de Frontenac"). Forts were also built in the older portions of New France that had not yet been settled.[[51]](#cite_note-55) Many of these forts were garrisoned by the [Troupes de la Marine](/wiki/Troupes_de_la_Marine "Troupes de la Marine"), the only regular soldiers in New France between 1683 and 1755.[[52]](#cite_note-56)

### Conquest of New France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Conquest of New France")]

This section is an excerpt from [Conquest of New France](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_France "Conquest of New France").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Conquest_of_New_France&action=edit)]

The [conquest of New France](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_France "Conquest of New France") (French: *La Conquête*) was the military conquest of New France by [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") during the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"). It started with a British campaign in 1758 and ended with the region being put under [a British military regime](/wiki/British_military_regime_in_New_France "British military regime in New France") between 1760 and 1763. Britain's acquisition of the [New France colony of Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)"), which the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") had established in 1535, became official with the 1763 [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") that concluded the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). The term is usually used when discussing the impact of the British conquest on the 70,000 French inhabitants, as well as on the [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"). At issue in popular and scholarly debate ever since is the British treatment of the French settler population along with the long-term historical impacts of the conquest.

## Fur trade and economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fur trade and economy")]

Main article: [Beaver Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars")

See also: [List of French forts in North America](/wiki/List_of_French_forts_in_North_America "List of French forts in North America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Monnaie_de_carte_du_Canada%2C_1714_%28recto-verso%29.png/250px-Monnaie_de_carte_du_Canada%2C_1714_%28recto-verso%29.png)](/wiki/File:Monnaie_de_carte_du_Canada,_1714_(recto-verso).png)

[Card money in New France](/wiki/Card_money_in_New_France "Card money in New France") had the same currency value in the colony as minted currency, c. 1714

According to the [staples thesis](/wiki/Staples_thesis "Staples thesis"), the economic development of New France was marked by the emergence of successive economies based on staple commodities, each of which dictated the political and cultural settings of the time. During the 16th and early 17th centuries New France's economy was heavily centered on its [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic "Atlantic") fisheries. This would change in the later half of the 17th and 18th centuries as [French](/wiki/France "France") settlement penetrated further into the continental interior.[[53]](#cite_note-57) Here French economic interests would shift and concentrate itself on the development of the [North American fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade"). It would soon become the new staple good that would strengthen and drive New France's economy, in particular that of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), for the next century.

The trading post of [Ville-Marie](/wiki/Ville-Marie,_Montreal "Ville-Marie, Montreal"), established on the current island of Montreal, quickly became the economic hub for the French fur trade. It achieved this in great part due to its particular location along the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"). From here a new economy emerged, one of size and density that provided increased economic opportunities for the inhabitants of New France. In December 1627 the [Company of New France](/wiki/Company_of_New_France "Company of New France") was recognized and given commercial rights to the gathering and export of [furs](/wiki/Furs "Furs") from French territories.[[54]](#cite_note-58) By trading with various indigenous populations and securing the main markets its power grew steadily for the next decade. As a result, it was able to set specific price points for furs and other valuable goods, often doing so to protect its economic hegemony over other trading partners and other areas of the economy.

The fur trade itself was based on a [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity") of small bulk but high value. Because of this it managed to attract increased attention and/or input capital that would otherwise be intended for other areas of the economy. The Montreal area witnessed a stagnant agricultural sector; it remained for the most part subsistence orientated with little or no trade purposes outside of the French [colony](/wiki/Colony "Colony"). This was a prime example of the handicapping effect the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") had on its neighbouring areas of the [economy](/wiki/Economy "Economy").[[55]](#cite_note-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Auberge_Du_Tresor.jpg/250px-Auberge_Du_Tresor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auberge_Du_Tresor.jpg)

[Company of New France](/wiki/Company_of_New_France "Company of New France") building in present-day [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City")

Nonetheless, by the beginning of the 1700s, the economic prosperity the fur trade stimulated slowly transformed Montreal. Economically, it was no longer a town of small traders or of fur fairs but rather a city of merchants and of bright lights. The primary sector of the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade"), the act of acquiring and the selling of the furs, quickly promoted the growth of complementary second and tertiary sectors of the economy. For instance a small number of tanneries was established in Montreal as well as a larger number of inns, taverns and markets that would support the growing number of inhabitants whose livelihood depended on the fur trade. Already by 1683 there were well over 140 families and there may have been as many as 900 people living in Montreal.

The founding of [John Law's Company](/wiki/John_Law%27s_Company "John Law's Company") in 1717, once again highlighted the economic importance of the fur trade.[[56]](#cite_note-60) This merchant association, like its predecessor the Compagnie des Cent Associés, regulated the fur trade to the best of its abilities imposing price points, supporting government sale taxes and combating black market practices. However, by the middle half of the 18th century the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") was in a slow decline.[[57]](#cite_note-illegalfurtrade-61)

The natural abundance of furs had passed and it could no longer meet market demand. This eventually resulted in the repeal of the 25 percent sales tax that had previously aimed at curbing the administrative costs New France had accumulated. In addition, dwindling supply increased black market trading. A greater number of indigenous groups and fur traders began circumventing [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and New France altogether; many began trading with either [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") or [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") merchants to the south.[[57]](#cite_note-illegalfurtrade-61)

By the end of French rule in New France in 1763, the fur trade had significantly lost its importance as the key staple good that supported much of New France's economy for more than the last century. Even so, it did serve as the fundamental force behind the establishment and vast growth of Montreal and the French colony.

### Coureurs des bois and voyageurs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Coureurs des bois and voyageurs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Arrival_of_Radisson_in_an_Indian_camp_1660_Charles_William_Jefferys.jpg/250px-Arrival_of_Radisson_in_an_Indian_camp_1660_Charles_William_Jefferys.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arrival_of_Radisson_in_an_Indian_camp_1660_Charles_William_Jefferys.jpg)

The arrival of [Radisson](/wiki/Pierre-Esprit_Radisson "Pierre-Esprit Radisson") in an [Amerindian](/wiki/Amerindian "Amerindian") camp in 1660

The [coureurs des bois](/wiki/Coureurs_des_bois "Coureurs des bois") were responsible for starting the flow of trade from [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), carrying [French](/wiki/France "France") goods into upper territories while indigenous people were bringing down their [furs](/wiki/Furs "Furs"). The coureurs traveled with intermediate trading tribes, and found that they were anxious to prevent French access to the more distant fur-hunting tribes. Still, the coureurs kept thrusting outwards using the [Ottawa River](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River") as their initial step upon the journey and keeping Montreal as their starting point.[[58]](#cite_note-Rich,_E._E._1966-62) The Ottawa River was significant because it offered a route that was practical for Europeans, by taking the traders northward out of the territory dominated by the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"). It was for this reason that Montreal and the Ottawa River was a central location of indigenous warfare and rivalry.

Montreal faced difficulties by having too many coureurs out in the woods. The furs coming down were causing an oversupply on the markets of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). This challenged the coureurs trade because they so easily evaded controls, monopolies, and taxation, and additionally because the coureurs trade was held to debauch both French and various indigenous groups. The coureur debauched Frenchmen by accustoming them to fully live with indigenous, and indigenous by trading on their desire for alcohol.[[58]](#cite_note-Rich,_E._E._1966-62)

The issues caused a great rift in the [colony](/wiki/Colony "Colony"), and in 1678, it was confirmed by a General Assembly that the trade was to be made in public so as to better assure the safety of the indigenous population. It was also forbidden to take spirits inland to trade with indigenous groups. However, these restrictions on the coureurs, for a variety of reasons, never worked. The [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") remained dependent on spirits, and increasingly in the hands of the coureurs who journeyed north in search of furs.[[58]](#cite_note-Rich,_E._E._1966-62)

As time passed, the Coureurs des bois were partially replaced by licensed fur trading endeavors, and the main canoe travel workers of those endeavors were called [voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs").

### Indigenous peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Indigenous peoples")]

The French and [Algonquins](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") first encountered one another in 1603 after [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") established France's first permanent North American settlement along the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"). In 1610, the Algonquins continued to solidify their relations with the French by guiding [Étienne Brûlé](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Br%C3%BBl%C3%A9 "Étienne Brûlé") into the interiors of Canada.

The relationship between the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") and the French first began in 1609, when [Samuel De Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") engaged in battle against the Iroquois. Champlain travelled from the [St. Lawrence Valley](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"), accompanied by his [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people"), [Montagnais](/wiki/Innu "Innu"), and [Huron](/wiki/Huron-Wendat_Nation "Huron-Wendat Nation") allies, and managed to kill three Iroquoian chiefs on [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain") with the first shots of his [arquebus](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus"). Subsequently, the two factions (Iroquois and French) were constantly at war with one another until the [Great Peace of Montréal](/wiki/Great_Peace_of_Montreal "Great Peace of Montreal") in 1701.[[59]](#cite_note-:0-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Beaver_wars_map.jpg/330px-Beaver_wars_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beaver_wars_map.jpg)

Map showing the approximate location of major [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") tribes and settlements[[60]](#cite_note-64)

The [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") were interested in exploiting the land through the fur trade as well as the timber trade later on. Despite having tools and guns, the French settlers were dependent on Indigenous people to survive in the difficult climate in this part of North America. Many settlers did not know how to survive through the winter; the Indigenous people showed them how to survive in the New World. They showed the settlers how to hunt for food and to use the furs for clothing that would protect them during the winter months.[[61]](#cite_note-65) Modern historians have highlighted that despite largely functional relations with indigenous peoples, administrators in France viewed co-operation as a wholly irritating task.[[62]](#cite_note-:3-66) Geographically removed from the colonies, Parisian courtiers viewed indigenous peoples as '*sauvages*', often criticising New French officials for even interacting with nations.[[62]](#cite_note-:3-66)

As the fur trade became the dominant economy in the New World, French voyageurs, trappers and hunters often married or formed relationships with Indigenous women. This allowed the French to develop relations with their wives' Indigenous nations, which in turn provided protection and access to their hunting and trapping grounds.

One specific Indigenous group borne of these relationships are the [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis") people, who are descendants of marriages between French men and Indigenous women. Their name originates from an old French term for “person of mixed parentage.”[[63]](#cite_note-67) At the beginning of the fur trade, these relationships were encouraged by the French as a way to encourage the First Nations to adopt French culture and solidify alliances, but as the Métis began to emerge as an independent culture around the 1700s, it began to be discouraged by the French.[[64]](#cite_note-68) Many Métis families moved to western Canada in response to this, as well as for other reasons, such as fur trading opportunities. One major settlement at this time was in the [Red River Valley](/wiki/Red_River_Valley "Red River Valley"), strategically placed in a significant area for the fur trade. This was the origin of the modern Métis nation, which was legally recognized by modern Canada as a protected Indigenous group in the [Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982"). Its prior legal history has its roots in acts such as the [Manitoba Act, 1870](/wiki/Manitoba_Act,_1870 "Manitoba Act, 1870"), which began to recognize the Métis nation as a separate group with various rights and protections, but was not supported by the vast majority of Métis as it removed many from land that was rightfully theirs.

The fur trade benefited Indigenous people as well. They traded furs for metal tools and other European-made items that made their lives easier. Tools such as knives, pots and kettles, nets, firearms and hatchets improved the general welfare of indigenous peoples. At the same time, while everyday life became easier, some traditional ways of doing things were abandoned or altered, and while Indigenous people embraced many of these implements and tools, they also were exposed to less vital trade goods, such as alcohol and sugar, sometimes with deleterious effects.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), like most tribes, began to rely on the importation of European goods, like firearms, which contributed significantly to a decrease in the beaver population of the [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley"). This decline resulted in the fur trade moving further north, along the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River").[[66]](#cite_note-70)

### Formal entry of England in New France area fur trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Formal entry of England in New France area fur trade")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Claude_Bernou_Carte_de_lAmerique_septentrionale.jpg/250px-Claude_Bernou_Carte_de_lAmerique_septentrionale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claude_Bernou_Carte_de_lAmerique_septentrionale.jpg)

1681 French map of the New World above the equator: New France and the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") in the north, with a dark line as the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") to the west in the [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country") and the mouth of the river (and future New Orleans) then *[terra incognita](/wiki/Terra_incognita "Terra incognita")*

Since [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson") had claimed [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"), and the surrounding lands for England in 1611, English colonists had begun expanding their boundaries across what is now the [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") north beyond the French-held territory of New France. In 1670, King Charles II of England issued a charter to Prince Rupert and "the Company of Adventurers of England trading into Hudson Bay" for an English monopoly in harvesting furs in [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land"), a portion of the land draining into [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"). This is the start of the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"), ironically aided by French *coureurs des bois*, [Pierre-Esprit Radisson](/wiki/Pierre-Esprit_Radisson "Pierre-Esprit Radisson") and [Médard des Groseilliers](/wiki/M%C3%A9dard_des_Groseilliers "Médard des Groseilliers"), frustrated with French license rules.[[67]](#cite_note-71)[[68]](#cite_note-72)[[69]](#cite_note-Fuchs-73) Now both France and England were formally in the Canadian fur trade.[[70]](#cite_note-Our_History:_People-74)

### The economy of *La Louisiane*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The economy of La Louisiane")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Fer_-_Le_Canada%2C_ou_Nouvelle_France%2C_la_Floride%2C_la_Virginie%2C_Pensilvanie%2C_Caroline.png/250px-Fer_-_Le_Canada%2C_ou_Nouvelle_France%2C_la_Floride%2C_la_Virginie%2C_Pensilvanie%2C_Caroline.png)](/wiki/File:Fer_-_Le_Canada,_ou_Nouvelle_France,_la_Floride,_la_Virginie,_Pensilvanie,_Caroline.png)

Map of Canada (New France) in 1703, showing full length of Mississippi River

The major commercial importance of the Louisiana Purchase territory was the Mississippi River. New Orleans, the largest and most important city in the territory, was the most commercial city in the United States until the Civil War, with most jobs there being related to trade and shipping; there was little manufacturing. The first commercial shipment to come down the Mississippi River was of deer and bear hides in 1705.[[71]](#cite_note-GarveyWidmer-75) The area, always loosely defined in those early times of European claims and settlements, extended as far east as the city that is now [Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Mobile,_Alabama#Colonial "Mobile, Alabama"), begun by French settlers in 1702.

The French (later Spanish) Louisiana Territory was owned by France for a number of years before the money-losing territory was transferred to French banker [Antoine Crozat](/wiki/Antoine_Crozat "Antoine Crozat") in 1713 for 15 years. After losing four times his investment, Crozat gave up his charter in 1717. Control of Louisiana and its 700 inhabitants was given to the Company of the Indies in 1719. The company conducted a major settlement program by recruiting European settlers to locate in the territory. Unemployed persons, convicts and prostitutes were also sent to the Louisiana Territory. After the bankruptcy of the company in 1720, control was returned to the king.[[71]](#cite_note-GarveyWidmer-75)[[72]](#cite_note-76)

[Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") saw little value in Louisiana, and to compensate Spain for its losses in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), he transferred Louisiana to his cousin [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain") in 1762. Louisiana remained under the control of Spain until it was demanded to be turned over to France by Napoleon. Although Louisiana was property of France by the [Third Treaty of San Ildefonso](/wiki/Third_Treaty_of_San_Ildefonso "Third Treaty of San Ildefonso") in 1800, Louisiana continued to be administered by Spain until the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") in 1803. Following the American acquisition of the territory, its population tripled between 1803 and Louisiana statehood in 1812.

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Jesuit missions in North America](/wiki/Jesuit_missions_in_North_America "Jesuit missions in North America")

Before the arrival of European colonists and explorers, First Nations followed a wide array of mostly [animistic religions](/wiki/Native_American_religion "Native American religion").[[73]](#cite_note-Tooker1979-77) As of 1607, official support for the first settlement at Port Royal was conditional based on efforts to convert the local First Nations people to Christianity.[[74]](#cite_note-78) The French who later settled along the shores of the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River"), specifically [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), including a number of [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuit_missions_in_North_America "Jesuit missions in North America") dedicated to converting the indigenous population; an effort that eventually proved successful.[[75]](#cite_note-FindlingThackeray2010-79)

Starting from 1627 in Quebec and 1659 in Acadia, all other forms of Christianity other than Roman Catholicism were forbidden to be worshipped or taught in New France and only French-born Roman Catholics were allowed to immigrate.[[76]](#cite_note-80)[[77]](#cite_note-81) This put an end to religious tolerance in the colony, notwithstanding the fact that both Port Royal and Quebec City were both founded by protestant [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") Pierre Dugua. After 1610, Dugua was banished from the French court due to his protestant faith and could only be represented through Champlain.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

As a result of official policy, the Catholic Church became the dominant force in New France.[[79]](#cite_note-83) In 1642, they sponsored a group of settlers, led by [Paul Chomedey de Maisonneuve](/wiki/Paul_Chomedey_de_Maisonneuve "Paul Chomedey de Maisonneuve"), who founded Ville-Marie, precursor to present-day [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), farther up the St. Lawrence.[[80]](#cite_note-Miquelon-84) Throughout the 1640s, Jesuit missionaries penetrated the Great Lakes region and converted many of the [Huron](/wiki/Wendat_people "Wendat people"). The missionaries came into conflict with the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), who frequently attacked Montreal.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Le_Grand_Voyage_du_Pays_des_Hurons_1632_Gabriel_Sagard.jpg/250px-Le_Grand_Voyage_du_Pays_des_Hurons_1632_Gabriel_Sagard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Le_Grand_Voyage_du_Pays_des_Hurons_1632_Gabriel_Sagard.jpg)

*Le Grand Voyage du Pays des [Hurons](/wiki/Hurons "Hurons")*, [Gabriel Sagard](/wiki/Gabriel_Sagard "Gabriel Sagard"), 1632

The presence of Jesuit missionaries in Huron society was nonnegotiable. The Huron relied on French goods to facilitate life and warfare. Because the French would refuse trade to all indigenous societies that denied relations with missionaries, the Huron had more of a propensity towards Christian conversion.[[81]](#cite_note-Seeman_2011_95-85) The Huron heavily relied on European goods to perform burial ceremonies known as [The Huron Feast of the Dead](/wiki/The_Huron_Feast_of_the_Dead "The Huron Feast of the Dead"). Trading with the French allowed for larger amounts of decorative goods to be buried during ceremonies as opposed to only a bare minimum.[[81]](#cite_note-Seeman_2011_95-85) With the growing epidemics and high number of deaths, the Huron could not afford to lose relations with the French, fearing to anger their ancestors.[[81]](#cite_note-Seeman_2011_95-85)

Jesuit missionaries explored the Mississippi River, including the [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"). Father [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") and explorer [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet") traveled in a small party, starting from Green Bay down the Wisconsin River to the Mississippi River, communicating with the tribes they met en route. Although Spanish trade goods had reached most of the indigenous peoples, these were the first Frenchmen to connect in the area named for the [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_Confederation "Illinois Confederation"), including the [Kaskaskia](/wiki/Kaskaskia "Kaskaskia"). They kept detailed records of what they saw and the people they met, sketching what they could, and mapped the Mississippi River in 1673.[[82]](#cite_note-Stelle2005-86) Their travels were described as first contacts with the indigenous peoples, though evidence of contact with Spanish from the south was clear.[[82]](#cite_note-Stelle2005-86)

Subsequent to the arrival of French children in Quebec in 1634, measles was also brought along with them, which quickly spread among the indigenous peoples. Jesuit priest [Jean de Brébeuf](/wiki/Jean_de_Br%C3%A9beuf "Jean de Brébeuf") described the symptoms as being severe. Brebeuf stated that the fearlessness of the indigenous peoples towards death upon this disease made them perfect candidates for conversion to Christianity.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeeman201150-87) The indigenous peoples believed that if they did not convert to Christianity, they would be exposed to the evil magic of the priests that caused the illness.[[81]](#cite_note-Seeman_2011_95-85)

Jesuit missionaries were troubled by the absence of patriarchy in indigenous communities. Indigenous women were highly regarded within their societies and participated in political and military decisions.[[84]](#cite_note-88) Jesuits attempted to eliminate the matriarchy and shift the powers of men and women to accommodate those of European societies. "In France, women are to be obedient to their masters, their husbands."[[85]](#cite_note-Catherine_Randall_2011-89) Jesuits would attempt to justify this to the indigenous women in hopes to enlighten them on proper European behavior. In response, Indigenous women grew worrisome of the presence of these missionaries fearing they would lose power and freedom within their communities.[[85]](#cite_note-Catherine_Randall_2011-89) By 1649, both the Jesuit mission and the Huron society were almost destroyed by [Iroquois invasions](/wiki/French_and_Iroquois_Wars "French and Iroquois Wars") (see [Canadian Martyrs](/wiki/Canadian_Martyrs "Canadian Martyrs")). In 1653, a peace invitation was extended by the [Onondaga Nation](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), one of the five nations of the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy"), to New France, and an expedition of Jesuits, led by [Simon Le Moyne](/wiki/Simon_Le_Moyne "Simon Le Moyne"), established [Sainte Marie de Ganentaa](/wiki/Sainte_Marie_among_the_Iroquois "Sainte Marie among the Iroquois") in 1656. The Jesuits were forced to abandon the mission by 1658, as hostilities with the Iroquois resumed.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

The second article of the charter of the *Compagnie des Cent-Associés* stated that New France could only be [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[87]](#cite_note-Zuidema2011-91) This resulted in [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") facing legal restrictions to enter the colony when [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu") transferred the control of the colony to *Compagnie des Cent-Associés* in 1627. Protestantism was then outlawed in [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and all its overseas possessions by the [Edict of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") in 1685.[[87]](#cite_note-Zuidema2011-91) In spite of that, approximately 15,000 [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") settled in New France by using socioeconomic pretexts while at the same time concealing their religious background.[[88]](#cite_note-Powell2009p-92)

The Huguenots (a name used to designate French-Speaking Protestants) were a mercantile group, originating from the coastal cities of North-Western France, and had a significant impact on the early development of New France, especially in the regions of Quebec and Acadia,[[89]](#cite_note-93) where many people still hold Huguenot surnames to this day.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Huguenots were famous for their large and interconnected trading and communication network that spanned throughout France, and most of her colonies.[[91]](#cite_note-Thompson_Rivers_University-95) This network was also known for trading with the Dutch Republic, and the Kingdom of England; two of France's most important rivals, that also happened to be Protestant nations.

Initially, King Henri IV recognised Protestants as a significant minority within France, and allowed them a certain degree of freedom within their religion. After several years of various skirmishes within Metropolitan France, the Huguenots were deemed to not be "faithful servants of the king", and their mercantile powers stripped, their trading network disbanded, and widespread governmental persecutory policies were enacted both within mainland France and Nouvelle France. In 1661, Louis XIV was able to enact self-rule as his regency ended, and he instituted a variety of anti-Protestant conventions throughout the greater French Empire. Under these new rules, Protestant children were forcibly converted to Catholicism, implemented direct governmental jurisdiction over what were formerly Huguenot-controlled trade routes, and labelled the Protestant communities throughout Nouvelle France (specifically Quebec and Acadia) as significant threats to the colonies, as they might sympathise with English Protestants competing in the same areas and trades. Eventually, Protestants were banned from settling in Nouvelle France, and the existing ones were only allowed to "summer" in the colonies, not "winter" there.[[91]](#cite_note-Thompson_Rivers_University-95)

## Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Health")]

Public health in New France was generally inadequate, despite the presence of a relatively good medical infrastructure.[[92]](#cite_note-96) An apothecary, [Louis Hébert](/wiki/Louis_H%C3%A9bert "Louis Hébert") In 1617 was engaged by [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") to serve the new colony as physician, surgeon and dispenser of medicines and herbs. After 1685 [Michel Sarrazin](/wiki/Michel_Sarrazin "Michel Sarrazin") and François Gaultier served as the King's physician of the colony. The colony opened small hospitals known as "[Hôtel-Dieu](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel-Dieu "Hôtel-Dieu")" in [Montréal](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al "Montréal"), [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") and [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières"). These institutions were managed by Catholic Church congregations included surgeons, doctors, apothecaries and healers. They provided care to settlers, soldiers, sailors, and occasionally Native populations. The Hôtel-Dieu in Quebec City cared for an average of 589 people per year from 1689, to 1759.[[93]](#cite_note-97) The Congregations organized and financed healthcare services. Their care aimed at both spiritual and physical healing.[[94]](#cite_note-98)

The colony faced high mortality rates due to infectious diseases. Epidemics imported from France were deadly. In 1687, measles and typhoid fever killed about 500 people. In 1702-1703, about 8% of the population died of smallpox. Major epidemics hit in 1715, 1731 and 1785.[[95]](#cite_note-99) Medical knowledge was limited, and treatments were often based on old French traditions. The towns were unsanitary, with unpaved streets, free-roaming animals, and lack of proper waste disposal. This led to frequent outbreaks of infectious diseases. Outside the towns the low population density mitigated the spread of diseases and conditions were better than France itself. The "Conseil supérieur" introduced laws to improve urban sanitation, such as requiring latrines and proper waste disposal.[[96]](#cite_note-100)[[97]](#cite_note-101) The British after 1759 made few changes in public health matters. They did bring in a few British military surgeons. Most of the French medical personnel remained and they followed the old routines, but no longer had replacements from Paris.[[98]](#cite_note-102)

## Judiciary of New France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Judiciary of New France")]

### Early history in New France (pre-1663)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Early history in New France (pre-1663)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Constitution-of-new-france-1759.png/250px-Constitution-of-new-france-1759.png)](/wiki/File:Constitution-of-new-france-1759.png)

A chart showing the political organization of New France, c. 1759

In the early stage of French settlement, legal matters fell within the [Governor of New France](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France")'s purview.[[99]](#cite_note-Lareau_244-103) Under this arrangement, legal disputes were settled in an incoherent fashion due to the Governor's arbitrariness in issuing verdicts.

Since 1640, a Seneschal (*sénéchal*), a Judge (*juge d'épée*, which literally means 'sword-bearing judge'), and a jurisdiction in [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières") were created.[[99]](#cite_note-Lareau_244-103) However, the Seneschal was under the oversight by the Governor, hence the Governor still had rather extensive control over legal matters in New France.[[99]](#cite_note-Lareau_244-103) In 1651, the [Company of New France](/wiki/Company_of_New_France "Company of New France") made the Great Seneschal (*Grand Sénéchal*) the chief justice.[[99]](#cite_note-Lareau_244-103) However, the [Island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal") had its special Governor at that time, who also administered justice on the Island, and had not handed over justice to the Grand Seneschal until 1652.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881246-104)

In practice, though, the Great Seneschal was awarded as an honorary title to the son of [Jean de Lauson](/wiki/Jean_de_Lauson "Jean de Lauson"), then Governor of New France; judicial functions were in fact carried out by the Seneschal's deputies.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881245-105) These deputies included such officials as the civil and criminal lieutenant general (*lieutenant général civil et criminel*), the special lieutenant (*lieutenant particulier*, acting as assistant royal judge), and the lieutenant fiscal (*lieutenant fiscal*, acting as tax magistrate).[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881245-105)

The Civil and Criminal Lieutenant General sat as judge in trials at first instance, whereas appeals would be adjudicated by the Governor, who held the sovereign right to settle final appeals on behalf of the French king. The Great Seneschal also had a magistrate in [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières"), as well as a [bailiff](/wiki/Bailiff "Bailiff") formed by the [Society of Priests of Saint Sulpice](/wiki/Society_of_Priests_of_Saint_Sulpice "Society of Priests of Saint Sulpice") on the [Island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal").[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Frontenac_with_the_Indians.jpg/250px-Frontenac_with_the_Indians.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frontenac_with_the_Indians.jpg)

[Governor Frontenac](/wiki/Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac "Louis de Buade de Frontenac") performing a tribal dance with indigenous allies

Apart from judicial responsibilities, the Great Seneschal was also in charge of convening local nobility in New France, as well as issuing declarations of war if necessary.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881246-104) However, such alternative role of the Great Seneschal was much weakened soon after by having the rights to declare war and to administer finances stripped off from the office because the French crown feared that colonial officers held too much authority.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881246-104)

### Legal reforms (1663)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Legal reforms (1663)")]

#### Royal judges and the Sovereign Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Royal judges and the Sovereign Council")]

On 13 October 1663, the royal court replaced the Seneschal Office (*sénéchaussée*). Canada was divided into three districts: the district of Quebec City, the district of [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières"), and the district of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881247-107) Each district had its own separate jurisdiction with a judge appointed by the Crown, known as the civil and criminal lieutenants general.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881247-107) They were responsible for all legal matters, civil and criminal, in each of the districts.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881247-107)

In addition to the royal judges, there were other judicial officers in each district. The clerk of court (registrar) was responsible for transcribing all court proceedings as well as other documents relevant to each of the cases.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881-106) The king's attorney (*procureur du roi*) was responsible for inquiring into the facts and preparing the case against the accused.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881249-108) In the districts of Quebec City and Montreal, the royal judges had special lieutenants to substitute them whenever they were absent or sick.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881249-108) Feudal courts heard minor cases.[[105]](#cite_note-109)[[106]](#cite_note-110)

The reform also brought the [Sovereign Council of New France](/wiki/Sovereign_Council_of_New_France "Sovereign Council of New France") (*Conseil souverain*) into existence, which was later renamed the Superior Council (*Conseil supérieur*). The Sovereign Council effectively acted as the functional equivalent of a Council of State (*Conseil d'État*) for New France, having the authority to hand down verdicts on final appeal.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881248-111) Initially, the Council convened once every week, and the quorum of the Sovereign Council was seven for criminal matters, or five for civil cases.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881248-111) The council's practices evolved over time. At the Sovereign Council there was a king's attorney-general (*procureur général du roi*) in charge of the similar tasks as the district king's attorneys.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881251-112) He was also responsible for supervising the king's attorneys' daily operations as well as execution of royal edicts and regulations passed by the council in their respective districts.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881252-113)

#### The Custom of Paris

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: The Custom of Paris")]

Main article: [Custom of Paris in New France](/wiki/Custom_of_Paris_in_New_France "Custom of Paris in New France")

In 1664, the [Custom of Paris](/wiki/Custom_of_Paris_in_New_France "Custom of Paris in New France") (*coutume de Paris*) was formally set as the main source of law for civil law in France's overseas empire. All royal judges and king's attorneys in New France had to be thoroughly familiar with this compilation of rules.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881248-111) The Custom governed various civil aspects of the daily life in New France, including property, marriage, inheritance, and so on.

##### Montreal Island: transition from feudal justice to royal justice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Montreal Island: transition from feudal justice to royal justice")]

The [Island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal") was a special case because its judiciary had been previously held by the Society of St-Sulpice. In 1663, Governor-General of New France [Augustin de Saffray de Mésy](/wiki/Augustin_de_Saffray_de_M%C3%A9sy "Augustin de Saffray de Mésy") originally considered appointing [Paul de Chomedey, Sieur de Maisonneuve](/wiki/Paul_de_Chomedey,_Sieur_de_Maisonneuve "Paul de Chomedey, Sieur de Maisonneuve") the Governor of the Island of Montreal and consolidating a royal jurisdiction on the island, but the plan garnered the [St-Sulpicians](/wiki/Society_of_Priests_of_Saint_Sulpice "Society of Priests of Saint Sulpice")' disapproval, who held the Island as its own [fiefdom](/wiki/Fief "Fief") and effectively acted as the island's governor.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881253-114) In other words, the [Sovereign Council](/wiki/Sovereign_Council_of_New_France "Sovereign Council of New France") had not been able to seize effective control over the legal matters of the Island; instead, the [St-Sulpicians](/wiki/Society_of_Priests_of_Saint_Sulpice "Society of Priests of Saint Sulpice") administered justice on the island.

It was not until 16 September 1666, that the St-Sulpicians finally handed over the justice of the Island of Montreal to the Intendant of New France.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881254-115) In 1693, the French king commanded the replacement of the ecclesiastical courts in Montreal with a royal court composed of one royal judge, with appeals going to the [Sovereign Council](/wiki/Sovereign_Council_of_New_France "Sovereign Council of New France"). The introduction of a royal court on the Montreal Island also resulted in the abolition of the feudal court in the fief of [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières") (then held by the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits")).[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881261-116)

##### Quebec: founding of the Provostry of Quebec

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Quebec: founding of the Provostry of Quebec")]

In the Quebec City district, the lower court (*tribunal antérieur*) was established in 1664 and had jurisdiction to try cases at first instance, but then it was abolished in 1674.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881264-117) The Sovereign Council appointed trial judges (*juges inférieurs*) to adjudicate cases at first instance until the [Provostry](/wiki/Provost_(civil) "Provost (civil)") of Quebec (*prévôté de Québec*) was created in May 1677.

The Provostry of Quebec was located in the Hall of Justice (*palais de justice*) in Quebec City and had only one royal judge, also known as the civil and criminal lieutenant general of Quebec City, who heard both civil and criminal cases, as well as district police.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881264-117) Additionally, a court clerk and a king's attorney were appointed to the court; if either of these two officers could not attend the trials due to illness or other untenable circumstances, the Intendant would appoint a temporary substitute.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881264-117)

### Criminal justice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Criminal justice")]

Main article: [Criminal justice in New France](/wiki/Criminal_justice_in_New_France "Criminal justice in New France")

In the early stages of French colonization, the execution of criminal justice in New France was rather arbitrary. The [Governor of New France](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France") served as the judge to the colonists as well as soldiers. He would announce his verdict at the presence of the chiefs of the Company of One Hundred Associates and that would be final.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881286-118)
