After the Sovereign Council was established in Quebec in 1663, the Council carried out criminal justice according to the general ordinances of France.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881286-118) In 1670, the [Criminal Ordinance](/wiki/Criminal_Ordinance_of_1670 "Criminal Ordinance of 1670") was enacted in New France by order of the French king as a codification of the previous criminal laws passed by the [Sovereign Council](/wiki/Sovereign_Council_of_New_France "Sovereign Council of New France").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881282-119)

### Special courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Special courts")]

#### Ecclesiastical court

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Ecclesiastical court")]

"Officialité (New France)" redirects here. For the ecclesiastical court, see [Ecclesiastical court](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_court "Ecclesiastical court").

The ecclesiastical court (*tribunal ecclésiastique*, or *Officialité*) was a special court for hearing first instance trials on both religious and secular affairs involving members of the Church.[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120) It first appeared in around 1660 but was not officially recognized by state authorities for it was not administered by a bishop, until 1684.[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120) Appeals from this court lay with the Sovereign Council.[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120)

#### Admiralty court

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Admiralty court")]

The court of admiralty was created on 12 January 1717 and was the last judicial body set up in Canada during the French colonial period.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881268-121) The court had a judge (also known as the lieutenant-general of the court) appointed by the French admiralty, a king's attorney, a clerk of court, and one or two bailiffs (*huissiers*).[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELareau1881269-122) The admiralty court was located in Quebec City and had jurisdiction over all of New France except [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") and [Louisbourg](/wiki/Fortress_of_Louisbourg "Fortress of Louisbourg").[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120) The court heard first instance trials on maritime affairs, including commerce and seamen's conduct.[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120) During wartime, it also commanded maritime police.[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120) Before 1717, the Quebec Provostry performed the duties of the admiralty court.[[116]](#cite_note-:1-120)

### Acadia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Acadia")]

Unlike Canada, [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia")'s judicial system was somewhat under-developed during the New France period. Prior to 1670, [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") was in a state of being contested between various European colonists. None of the European countries—France, England, the Netherlands—were able to put in place a stable jurisdiction there.

In 1670, France regained control of Acadia and appointed Mathieu de Goutin as the Civil and Criminal Lieutenant (*lieutenant civil et criminel*) of Acadia.[[119]](#cite_note-123) Simultaneously, the [Governor of Acadia](/wiki/Governor_of_Acadia "Governor of Acadia") was set up and his job was primarily the defense of [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") from English attacks.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanderlinden200455-124) The Civil and Criminal Lieutenant was essentially supervised by the Governor, who held superior judicial authority over the Lieutenant, but for most of the time would let the Lieutenant mediate and decide legal affairs.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanderlinden2004201-125)

Due to the situation in [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") as a small settlement of around 399 settlers in 1670–71, vulnerable to foreign invasion, courts were minimal, consisting of only a Civil and Criminal Lieutenant and a king's attorney.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanderlinden200455-124) There was not an official court in [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), although the king's attorney of Acadia performed very similar duties as his counterpart in New France.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanderlinden200469-126) Yet since [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") never actually had a court, there was no clerk of court; instead, trials were recorded by a local [notary](/wiki/Civil_law_notary "Civil law notary").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanderlinden200469-126) It is difficult to trace the judicial history of French Acadia as the relevant archives were destroyed in a fire in 1708.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanderlinden200469-126)

## Military conflicts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Military conflicts")]

Main articles: [French and Indian Wars](/wiki/French_and_Indian_Wars "French and Indian Wars"), [Father Rale's War](/wiki/Father_Rale%27s_War "Father Rale's War"), and [Father Le Loutre's War](/wiki/Father_Le_Loutre%27s_War "Father Le Loutre's War")

Further information: [Military of New France](/wiki/Military_of_New_France "Military of New France")

The presence of settlers, of businesses from several European countries harvesting furs, along with the interests of the indigenous people in this new competition for North American resources set the scene for significant military conflicts among all parties in New France beginning in 1642, and ending with the Seven Years' War, 1756–1763.

### Iroquois attacks against Montreal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Iroquois attacks against Montreal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/db/Battle_of_Long_Sault_1660.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Long_Sault_1660.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Long_Sault_1660.jpg)

Engraving depicting Adam Dollard with a keg of gunpowder above his head, during the [Battle of Long Sault](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Sault "Battle of Long Sault")

[Ville-Marie](/wiki/Ville-Marie,_Quebec "Ville-Marie, Quebec") was a noteworthy site for it was the center of defence against the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), the point of departure for all western and northern journeys, and the meeting point to which the trading Indians brought their annual furs. This placed Ville-Marie, later known as [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), at the forefront against the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), which resulted in its trade being easily and frequently interrupted. The Iroquois were in alliance with the Dutch and English,[[123]](#cite_note-Inc.2010-127) which allowed them to interrupt the French fur trade and send the furs down the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to the Dutch and English traders.[[58]](#cite_note-Rich,_E._E._1966-62)

This also put the Iroquois at warfare against the [Hurons](/wiki/Hurons "Hurons"), the [Algonquians](/wiki/Algonquians "Algonquians"), and any other tribes that were in alliance with the French. If the Iroquois could destroy New France and its Indian allies, they would be able to trade freely and profitably with the Dutch and English on the Hudson River.[[124]](#cite_note-AyersGould-128) The Iroquois formally attacked the settlement at today's [Quebec City](/wiki/History_of_Quebec_City "History of Quebec City") in its foundation year of 1642, and in almost every subsequent year thereafter.[[125]](#cite_note-Otterbein2004-129) A militant theocracy maintained Montreal. In 1653 and 1654, reinforcements arrived at Montreal, which allowed the Iroquois to be halted.[[126]](#cite_note-Adams2013-130)[*[self-published source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In that year the Iroquois made peace with the French.[[58]](#cite_note-Rich,_E._E._1966-62)

[Adam Dollard des Ormeaux](/wiki/Adam_Dollard_des_Ormeaux "Adam Dollard des Ormeaux"), a colonist and soldier of New France, was a notable figure regarding the Iroquois attacks against Montreal. The Iroquois soon resumed their assaults against Montreal, and the few settlers of Montreal fell almost completely to hostile Iroquois forces. The [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") did not use typical raiding tactics of moving swiftly and silently. Instead, they captured individuals and brought them back to their own territory. Women and children were made a part of the village, and men encountered slow torturous deaths. In the 1660s, warfare changed, and France began to counterattack. Professional French soldiers had arrived in the New World for the first time, and [Alexandre de Prouville](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Prouville_de_Tracy "Alexandre de Prouville de Tracy") led them to invade Iroquois territory.[[127]](#cite_note-:2-131) In the spring of 1660, Adam Dollard des Ormeaux led a small militia consisting of 16 men from Montreal against a much larger Iroquois force at the [Battle of Long Sault](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Sault "Battle of Long Sault") on the [Ottawa River](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River").[[128]](#cite_note-Gough2010n-132) They succeeded in turning back the Iroquois invasion and are responsible for saving Montreal from destruction.[[129]](#cite_note-Del?geBrierley2011-133) They were able to take [Chief Canaqueese](/wiki/Chief_Canaqueese "Chief Canaqueese") as a prisoner, and in September 1660, the French returned and burned [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") homes and crops. Later that Winter, many Iroquois died due to starvation, and the Iroquois finally agreed to peace which lasted roughly twenty years.[[127]](#cite_note-:2-131) The encounter between Ormeaux and the Iroquois is of significance because it dissuaded the Iroquois from further attacks against Montreal.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

### King William's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: King William's War")]

Main article: [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/QueenAnnesWarBefore.svg/250px-QueenAnnesWarBefore.svg.png)](/wiki/File:QueenAnnesWarBefore.svg)

Map of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") in 1702 showing forts, towns and (in solid colors) areas occupied by European settlements

In 1688, [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War") began and the English and Iroquois launched a major assault on New France, after many years of small skirmishes throughout the English and French territories. New France and the [Wabanaki Confederacy](/wiki/Wabanaki_Confederacy "Wabanaki Confederacy") were able to thwart New England expansion into Acadia, whose border New France defined as the [Kennebec River](/wiki/Kennebec_River "Kennebec River") in southern Maine.[[131]](#cite_note-Williamson-135)[[132]](#cite_note-Griffiths-136)[[133]](#cite_note-Campbell-137) [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War") ended in 1697, but a second war ([Queen Anne's War](/wiki/Queen_Anne%27s_War "Queen Anne's War")) broke out in 1702. Quebec survived the English invasions of both these wars, and during the wars France seized many of the English [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") fur trading centres on [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") including [York Factory](/wiki/York_Factory "York Factory"), which the French renamed *Fort Bourbon*.

### Queen Anne's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Queen Anne's War")]

Main article: [Queen Anne's War](/wiki/Queen_Anne%27s_War "Queen Anne's War")

While Acadia defeated an English invasion attempt during [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War"), the colony was occupied by the British during Queen Anne's War. The final [Conquest of Acadia](/wiki/Siege_of_Port_Royal_(1710) "Siege of Port Royal (1710)") happened in 1710. In 1713, peace came to New France with the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht_(1713) "Treaty of Utrecht (1713)").[[134]](#cite_note-138) Although the treaty turned Hudson Bay, [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)") and part of [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") (peninsular [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia")) over to Great Britain, France remained in control of [Île Royale](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_Royale_(New_France) "Île Royale (New France)") ([Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island")) (which also administered Île Saint-Jean ([Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"))). The northern part of [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), what is today [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") and [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), remained contested territory. Construction of [Fortress Louisbourg](/wiki/Fortress_Louisbourg "Fortress Louisbourg") on Île Royale, a French military stronghold intended to protect the approaches to the St. Lawrence River settlements, began in 1719.[[135]](#cite_note-139)

### Father Rale's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Father Rale's War")]

Main article: [Father Rale's War](/wiki/Father_Rale%27s_War "Father Rale's War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Death_of_Father_Sebastian_Rale_of_the_Society_of_Jesus.jpg/250px-Death_of_Father_Sebastian_Rale_of_the_Society_of_Jesus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Death_of_Father_Sebastian_Rale_of_the_Society_of_Jesus.jpg)

An 1850s depiction of the death of the French [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") priest [Sébastien Rale](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Rale "Sébastien Rale") during [Father Rale's War](/wiki/Father_Rale%27s_War "Father Rale's War")

In Acadia, however, war continued. [Father Rale's War](/wiki/Father_Rale%27s_War "Father Rale's War") (1722–1725) was a series of battles between New England and the [Wabanaki Confederacy](/wiki/Wabanaki_Confederacy "Wabanaki Confederacy"), who were allied with New France. New France and the Wabanaki Confederacy defended against the expansion of New England settlements into Acadia, whose border New France defined as the [Kennebec River](/wiki/Kennebec_River "Kennebec River") in southern Maine.[[131]](#cite_note-Williamson-135)[[132]](#cite_note-Griffiths-136)[[133]](#cite_note-Campbell-137) After the New England [Conquest of Acadia](/wiki/Siege_of_Port_Royal_(1710) "Siege of Port Royal (1710)") in 1710, mainland Nova Scotia was under the control of New England, but both present-day [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") and virtually all of present-day Maine remained contested territory between New England and New France. To secure New France's claim to the region, it established [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") missions among the three largest indigenous villages in the region: one on the Kennebec River ([Norridgewock](/wiki/Norridgewock "Norridgewock")); one further north on the [Penobscot River](/wiki/Penobscot_River "Penobscot River") ([Penobscot](/wiki/Penobscot_Indian_Island_Reservation "Penobscot Indian Island Reservation")) and one on the [Saint John River](/wiki/Saint_John_River_(Bay_of_Fundy) "Saint John River (Bay of Fundy)") ([Medoctec](/wiki/Meductic_Indian_Village_/_Fort_Meductic "Meductic Indian Village / Fort Meductic")).[[136]](#cite_note-140)[[137]](#cite_note-Grenier-141)

The war began on two fronts: when New England pushed its way through Maine and when New England established itself at [Canso, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Canso,_Nova_Scotia "Canso, Nova Scotia"). As a result of the war, Maine fell to the New Englanders with the defeat of Father [Sébastien Rale](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Rale "Sébastien Rale") at Norridgewock and the subsequent retreat of the indigenous peoples from the Kennebec and Penobscot rivers to [St. Francis](/wiki/Saint-Fran%C3%A7ois-du-Lac,_Quebec "Saint-François-du-Lac, Quebec") and [Becancour, Quebec](/wiki/B%C3%A9cancour,_Quebec "Bécancour, Quebec").[[e]](#cite_note-142)

### King George's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: King George's War")]

Main article: [King George's War](/wiki/King_George%27s_War "King George's War")

Peace lasted in Canada until 1744, when news of the outbreak of the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") ([King George's War](/wiki/King_George%27s_War "King George's War") in North America) reached Fort Louisbourg. The French forces went on the attack first in a failed attempt to capture [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal "Annapolis Royal"), the capital of British Nova Scotia. In 1745, [William Shirley](/wiki/William_Shirley "William Shirley"), governor of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), led a counterattack on Louisbourg. Both France and New France were unable to relieve the siege, and Louisbourg fell to the British. With the famed [Duc d'Anville Expedition](/wiki/Duc_d%27Anville_Expedition "Duc d'Anville Expedition"), France attempted to retake Acadia and the fortress in 1746 but failed. The fortress was returned to France under the [Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1748) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748)"), but the peace treaty, which restored all colonial borders to their pre-war status, did little to end the lingering enmity between France, Britain, and their respective colonies, nor did it resolve any territorial disputes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Father Le Loutre's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Father Le Loutre's War")]

Main article: [Father Le Loutre's War](/wiki/Father_Le_Loutre%27s_War "Father Le Loutre's War")

Within Acadia and Nova Scotia, [Father Le Loutre's War](/wiki/Father_Le_Loutre%27s_War "Father Le Loutre's War") (1749–1755) began with the British founding of [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Halifax Regional Municipality"). During Father Le Loutre's War, New France established three forts along the border of present-day New Brunswick to protect it from a New England attack from Nova Scotia. The war continued until British [victory at Fort Beausejour](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Beaus%C3%A9jour "Battle of Fort Beauséjour"), which dislodged Father Le Loutre from the region, thereby ending his alliance with the Maliseet, [Acadians](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians") and [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq "Mi'kmaq").[[137]](#cite_note-Grenier-141)

### French and Indian War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: French and Indian War")]

Main articles: [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") and [Expulsion of the Acadians](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Nouvelle-France_map-en.svg/250px-Nouvelle-France_map-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nouvelle-France_map-en.svg)

Map of territorial claims in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") by 1750, before the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), which was part of the greater worldwide conflict known as the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") (1756 to 1763). Possessions of Britain (pink), France (blue), and Spain. (White border lines mark later Canadian Provinces and US States for reference)

[Fort Duquesne](/wiki/Fort_Duquesne "Fort Duquesne"), located at the confluence of the [Allegheny](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") and [Monongahela Rivers](/wiki/Monongahela_River "Monongahela River") at the site of present-day [Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pittsburgh,_Pennsylvania "Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania"), guarded the most important strategic location in the west at the time of the Seven Years' War. It was built to ensure that the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") valley remained under French control. A small colonial force from Virginia began a fort here, but a French force under [Claude-Pierre Pécaudy de Contrecœur](/wiki/Claude-Pierre_P%C3%A9caudy_de_Contrec%C5%93ur "Claude-Pierre Pécaudy de Contrecœur") drove them off in April 1754. New France claimed this as part of their colony, and the French were anxious to keep the British from encroaching on it. The French built Fort Duquesne here to serve as a military stronghold and as a base for developing trade and strengthening military alliances with the indigenous peoples of the area.

In 1755, General [Edward Braddock](/wiki/Edward_Braddock "Edward Braddock") led [an expedition](/wiki/Braddock_Expedition "Braddock Expedition") against Fort Duquesne, and although they were numerically superior to the French militia and their Indian allies, Braddock's army was routed and Braddock was killed.[[138]](#cite_note-WDL-143) Later that same year at the Battle of Lake George, the British General William Johnson with a force of 1700 American and Iroquois troops defeated a French force of 2800 French and Canadians and 700 [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") led by Baron Dieskau (Military commander of New France).

The fight for control over Ohio Country led to the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), which began as the North American phase of the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") (which did not technically begin in Europe until 1756). The war began with the defeat of a Virginia militia contingent led by Colonel [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") by the French [troupes de la marine](/wiki/Troupes_de_la_marine "Troupes de la marine") in the [Ohio valley](/wiki/Ohio_Country "Ohio Country"). As a result of that defeat, the British decided to prepare the conquest of Quebec City, the capital of New France. The British defeated France in Acadia in the [Battle of Fort Beausejour](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Beausejour "Battle of Fort Beausejour") (1755) and then [Île Royale](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_Royale_(New_France) "Île Royale (New France)") ([Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island")) (which also administered Île Saint-Jean ([Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island")) with the [Siege of Louisbourg (1758)](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1758) "Siege of Louisbourg (1758)").

Throughout the war, the British deported the Acadians to the Thirteen Colonies and Europe, which the Acadian militias resisted with assistance from Mi'kmaq and Malisteet forces. The [Great Upheaval](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians") continued from 1755 to 1764. In 1756, a large force of French, Canadians, and their Native American allies led by the Marquis de Montcalm launched an attack against the key British post at Fort Oswego on Lake Ontario from Fort Frontenac and forced the garrison to surrender. The following year Montcalm with a huge force of 7,200 French and Canadian troops and 2,400 Native Americans laid siege to Fort William Henry on the southern shores of Lake George, and after three weeks of fighting the British commander Monroe surrendered. Montcalm gave him honorable terms to return to England and not to fight for 18 months. And yet, when the British force with civilians was three miles from the fort, the Native American allies massacred about 1,100 of the 1,500 strong force.

In 1758, the French suffered a defeat when the British captured the fortress city of Louisborg in July, while gaining a victory at [Fort Carillon](/wiki/Fort_Carillon "Fort Carillon") in July. The [Battle of Carillon](/wiki/Battle_of_Carillon "Battle of Carillon") was fought at the fortress of the same name, which is located on a strip of land between Lake Champlain and Lake George, and which was defended by 3,400 French regular troops and marines, with minimal support from militia and indigenous peoples. The battle was the largest seen in North America up to that time, as General James Abercrombie assembled a force of 16,200 British, American, and Iroquois troops. A spirited French defense led the British to withdraw after a fierce battle on the 8th of July, 1758. During the battle, the British suffered 2,200 casualties and lost several artillery pieces, while the French received roughly 104 killed and 273 wounded.

While the British [Conquest of Acadia](/wiki/Siege_of_Port_Royal_(1710) "Siege of Port Royal (1710)") happened in 1710, the French continued to remain a significant force in the region with [Fort Beausejour](/wiki/Fort_Beausejour "Fort Beausejour") and [Fortress Louisbourg](/wiki/Fortress_Louisbourg "Fortress Louisbourg"). The dominant population in the region remained Acadian, that is to say, not British. In 1755, the British were successful in the [Battle of Beausejour](/wiki/Battle_of_Beausejour "Battle of Beausejour") and immediately after began the [expulsion of the Acadians](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians").

In the meantime the French continued to explore westwards and expand their trade alliances with indigenous peoples. [Fort de la Corne](/wiki/Fort_de_la_Corne "Fort de la Corne") was built in 1753, by [Louis de la Corne, Chevalier de la Corne](/wiki/Louis_de_la_Corne,_Chevalier_de_la_Corne "Louis de la Corne, Chevalier de la Corne") just east of the [Saskatchewan River Forks](/wiki/Saskatchewan_River_Forks "Saskatchewan River Forks") in what is today the Canadian province of [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). This was the furthest westward outpost of the French Empire in North America to be established before its fall.

## Treaties of cession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Treaties of cession")]

Further information: [Conquest of New France (1758-1760)](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_France_(1758-1760) "Conquest of New France (1758-1760)")

In 1758, British forces again [captured Louisbourg](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1758) "Siege of Louisbourg (1758)"), allowing them to blockade the entrance to the St. Lawrence River. This proved decisive in the war. In 1759, the British besieged Quebec by sea, and an army under General [James Wolfe](/wiki/James_Wolfe "James Wolfe") defeated the French under General [Louis-Joseph de Montcalm](/wiki/Louis-Joseph_de_Montcalm "Louis-Joseph de Montcalm") at the [Battle of the Plains of Abraham](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Plains_of_Abraham "Battle of the Plains of Abraham") in September. The garrison in Quebec surrendered on 18 September, and by the next year New France had been conquered by the British after [the attack on Montreal](/wiki/Montreal_Campaign "Montreal Campaign"), which had refused to acknowledge the fall of Canada. The last French governor-general of New France, [Pierre François de Rigaud, Marquis de Vaudreuil-Cavagnal](/wiki/Pierre_Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Rigaud,_Marquis_de_Vaudreuil-Cavagnal "Pierre François de Rigaud, Marquis de Vaudreuil-Cavagnal"), surrendered to British Major General [Jeffery Amherst](/wiki/Jeffery_Amherst,_1st_Baron_Amherst "Jeffery Amherst, 1st Baron Amherst") on 8 September 1760. France formally ceded Canada to the British in the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), signed 10 February 1763.[[139]](#cite_note-144)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

Main articles: [Canada under British Imperial control](/wiki/Canada_under_British_Imperial_control "Canada under British Imperial control"), [Louisiana (New Spain)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)"), and [Colonial history of the United States](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/NorthAmerica1762-83.png/250px-NorthAmerica1762-83.png)](/wiki/File:NorthAmerica1762-83.png)

Map showing British territorial gains following the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") in pink, and Spanish territorial gains after the [Treaty of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fontainebleau_(1762) "Treaty of Fontainebleau (1762)") in yellow

The expelled [Acadians](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians") were initially dispersed across much of eastern North America (including the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies")) and some were sent to France. Many eventually settled in Quebec or Louisiana, while others returned to the regions of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") and [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"). [Chéticamp, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Ch%C3%A9ticamp,_Nova_Scotia "Chéticamp, Nova Scotia"), and the [Magdalen Islands](/wiki/Magdalen_Islands#History "Magdalen Islands") have significant communities. In Louisiana their descendants became known as the [Cajuns](/wiki/Cajuns "Cajuns"), a corruption of the French *Acadiens*.

By the mid-1700s, the [French settlers were well established](/wiki/List_of_French_forts_in_North_America "List of French forts in North America") with a population around 70,000, mainly due to natural increase.[[140]](#cite_note-StatCan2009-145)[[141]](#cite_note-Preston2009-146) The European population had grown slowly under French rule.[[15]](#cite_note-Powell2009t-19)[[142]](#cite_note-Dale2004b-147)[[143]](#cite_note-FindlingThackeray2011-148) The British [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") to the south along the Atlantic coast grew in population from natural increase and more new settlers from Europe. By 1760, almost 1.6 million people lived in the British colonies, a ratio of approximately twenty-three to one compared to New France.[[144]](#cite_note-BogueAnderton2010-149) The population of the New England colonies alone in 1760 was nearly 450,000.

French culture and religion remained dominant in most of the former territory of New France until the arrival of British settlers led to the later creation of [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") (today [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")) and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"). The [Louisiana Territory](/wiki/Louisiana_Territory "Louisiana Territory"), under [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") control since the end of the Seven Years' War, remained off-limits to settlement from the thirteen American colonies.

Twelve years after the British defeated the French, the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") broke out in the Thirteen Colonies. Many French Canadians would take part in the war, including Major [Clément Gosselin](/wiki/Cl%C3%A9ment_Gosselin "Clément Gosselin") and Admiral [Louis-Philippe de Vaudreuil](/wiki/Louis-Philippe_de_Vaudreuil "Louis-Philippe de Vaudreuil"). After the British surrender at [Yorktown](/wiki/Battle_of_Yorktown_(1781) "Battle of Yorktown (1781)") in 1781, the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles_(1783) "Treaty of Versailles (1783)") gave all former British claims in New France below the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") into the possession of the nascent [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). A Franco-Spanish alliance treaty returned Louisiana to France in 1801, but French leader [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") sold it to the United States in the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") in 1803, ending French colonial efforts in North America.

The portions of the former New France that remained under British rule were administered as [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada"), 1791–1841, and then those regions were merged as the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada") during 1841–1867, when the passage of the [British North America Act 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act_1867 "British North America Act 1867") instituted [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") for most of British North America and established French-speaking Quebec (the former Lower Canada) as one of the original provinces of the [Dominion of Canada](/wiki/Confederation_of_Canada "Confederation of Canada"). The former French colony of Acadia was first designated the [Colony of Nova Scotia](/wiki/Colony_of_Nova_Scotia "Colony of Nova Scotia") but shortly thereafter the [Colony of New Brunswick](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Brunswick "Colony of New Brunswick"), which then included Prince Edward Island, was split off from it.

In Canada, the legacy of New France can be seen in the enduring [Francophone identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity#French_Canadians_and_Identity_in_English_Canada "Canadian identity") of its descendants, which has led to [institutional bilingualism](/wiki/Bilingualism_in_Canada "Bilingualism in Canada") in Canada as a whole.

The French [overseas collectivity](/wiki/Overseas_collectivity "Overseas collectivity") of [Saint Pierre and Miquelon](/wiki/Saint_Pierre_and_Miquelon "Saint Pierre and Miquelon") (French: Collectivité territoriale de Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon), consisting of a group of small islands 25 kilometres (16 mi; 13 nmi) off the coast of [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"), were ceded to France by Britain in 1763 to assist with existing French fishing rights: the islands are not remnants of New France.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Historiography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_France&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Historiography")]

The Conquest (referring to the fall of New France to the British, and specifically the events of 1759–60) has always been a central and contested theme of Canadian memory. Some Anglophone historians portray the Conquest as a victory for "British military, political and economic superiority" and argue that it ultimately brought benefits to the French settlers.[[145]](#cite_note-Jaenen-150) However, Cornelius Jaenen notes that French-Canadian historians remain strongly divided on the subject. One group sees it as a highly negative economic, political and ideological disaster that threatened a way of life with materialism and Protestantism. At the other pole are those historians who see the positive benefit of enabling the preservation of language, and religion and traditional customs under British rule.[[145]](#cite_note-Jaenen-150) French-Canadian debates have escalated since the 1960s, as the conquest is seen as a pivotal moment in the history of Québec's nationalism. Francophone historian Jocelyn Létourneau suggested in 2009, that today, "1759 does not belong primarily to a past that we might wish to study and understand, but, rather, to a present and a future that we might wish to shape and control."[[146]](#cite_note-151)

The enduring contestation of the legacy of the Conquest can be exemplified by an episode in 2009, when an attempt to commemorate the 250th anniversary of the battle of the Plains of Abraham was cancelled. The explanation for the cancellation was that it was over security concerns, but activist Sylvain Rocheleau stated, "[I think] they had to cancel the event because it was insulting a majority of Francophones. They had to cancel it because it was a bad idea.".[[147]](#cite_note-152)
